title: To my Dear and Loving Husband by Anne Bradstreet — Deep Poetry Lines
content:
If ever two were one, then surely we.
If ever man were lov'd by wife, then thee.
If ever wife was happy in a man,
Compare with me, ye women, if you can.
I prize thy love more than whole Mines of gold
Or all the riches that the East doth hold.
My love is such that Rivers cannot quench,
Nor ought but love from thee give recompetence.
meaning: "To my Dear and Loving Husband" by Anne Bradstreet is a deep poem consisting of 12 lines. The poet explores themes of deep through vivid imagery and emotional depth. Beginning with "If ever two were one, then surely we. If ever man were lov'd by wife, then thee....", this piece captures the essence of human experience and invites reflection on the deeper currents of life and feeling. This work is part of Anne Bradstreet's celebrated body of poetry that continues to resonate with readers worldwide.
